title: Känguru der Mathematik 2019 — Felix (Pré-Écolier, Grades 1-2)
source_organization: Känguru der Mathematik (Austria) / Kangourou sans Frontières
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper (kaenguru.at, via matematica.pt). For personal educational practice only.
answer_source: Official 2019_Answers.pdf answer table (Felix row), read and verified.

questions.question_text: Which of these clouds contains only numbers that are smaller than 7?
questions.solution: Check each cloud and reject it the moment it has a number 7 or bigger. Cloud D holds 3, 2, 1 and 5 — all smaller than 7.
solution_steps: A number must be smaller than 7 (so 7 itself is not allowed).
Reject any cloud containing 7, 8, 9 or 10.
Only cloud D (3, 2, 1, 5) has every number below 7.

questions.question_text: Which of the 5 pictures shows a part of this chain?
questions.solution: Read the colours around the loop chain in order. The short strip whose colour sequence appears somewhere in that loop is option C.
solution_steps: Note the repeating colour order around the loop.
Compare each short strip to a stretch of that loop.
Only option C matches a part of the chain.

questions.question_text: Mother kangaroo and her son Max together weigh 60 kg. The mother on her own weighs 52 kg. How heavy is Max?
questions.solution: Take the mother's weight away from the total: 60 − 52 = 8 kg. So Max weighs 8 kg.
solution_steps: Together they weigh 60 kg.
The mother alone is 52 kg.

questions.question_text: There are 12 children in front of a zoo. Susi is the 7th from the front and Kim is the 2nd from the back. How many children are there between Susi and Kim?
questions.solution: Kim is 2nd from the back of 12, so Kim is the 11th from the front. Between position 7 (Susi) and position 11 (Kim) are positions 8, 9 and 10 — that is 3 children.
solution_steps: Kim is 2nd from the back of 12 → 11th from the front.
Children between positions 7 and 11 are at 8, 9, 10.
That is 3 children.

questions.question_text: Jörg is sorting his socks. Two socks with the same number make one pair. Some numbers are printed upside down. How many pairs can he find?
questions.solution: Read each sock's number carefully, turning the upside-down ones the right way up first. Matching equal numbers gives 5 pairs.
solution_steps: Turn each upside-down number the right way up before reading it.
Match socks that show the same number.
There are 5 matching pairs.

questions.question_text: Five equally big square cards are placed on a table on top of each other, making the picture shown. The cards are collected up from top to bottom. In which order are they collected?
questions.solution: The card lying fully on top (covering all others) is taken first, then the next one that is no longer covered, and so on. Following which card covers which, the order is 5, 2, 3, 1, 4.
solution_steps: The top card is the one not covered by any other.
Remove it, then take the next uncovered card, and repeat.
The order from top to bottom is 5-2-3-1-4.

questions.question_text: There are bactrian camels with 2 humps and dromedaries with 1 hump. Exactly 10 camels live in a zoo and together they have 14 humps. How many bactrian camels are there?
questions.solution: If all 10 camels had 1 hump there would be 10 humps. The extra 14 − 10 = 4 humps each come from a bactrian camel's second hump, so there are 4 bactrian camels.
solution_steps: If every camel had 1 hump: 10 humps.
Extra humps: 14 − 10 = 4, one per bactrian camel.
So there are 4 bactrian camels.

questions.question_text: The floor of a room is covered with equally big rectangular tiles. One length of 1 m is marked. How long is the room?
questions.solution: Use the marked 1 m to find the size of one tile, then count how many tile-lengths fit along the room. That gives a room length of 12 m.
solution_steps: The 1 m mark tells you one tile's size.
Count how many tile lengths fit along the room's side.
The room is 12 m long.

questions.question_text: The mouse may only move to neighbouring fields in the direction of the arrows (right and down). How many paths are there from the mouse to the cheese?
questions.solution: On the 3-by-3 grid the mouse only moves right or down. Counting all such routes from the top-left mouse to the bottom-right cheese gives 6 paths.
solution_steps: The mouse can only step right or down.
Count the right/down routes across the grid (add paths square by square).
There are 6 different paths.

questions.question_text: Which of the figures can be cut into these 3 pieces?
questions.solution: Fit the three grey pieces together without overlaps. They join up to form the heart shape, option C.
solution_steps: Try to combine the three pieces edge to edge.
Check which whole figure they fill exactly.
They make the heart, option C.

questions.question_text: The giants insert half of the flagpole into the top of the sandcastle. The highest point of the pole is 16 m above the floor and the lowest point is 6 m. How high is the sandcastle?
questions.solution: The whole pole is 16 − 6 = 10 m, so half of it (the buried part) is 5 m. The sandcastle's top is where the pole enters: 6 m (pole bottom) + 5 m = 11 m.
solution_steps: Pole length = 16 − 6 = 10 m, so the buried half = 5 m.
The pole's bottom is at 6 m; the pole enters the castle 5 m above that.
Sandcastle height = 6 + 5 = 11 m.

questions.question_text: Three children change a row of white, grey and black squares. Anni replaces all black with white, then Bob replaces all grey with black, then Chris replaces all white with grey. Which picture have they created?
questions.solution: Apply the three steps in order to the row. After black→white, then grey→black, then white→grey, every original square ends up as the colour shown in option A.
solution_steps: Step 1: every black becomes white.
Step 2: every grey becomes black. Step 3: every white becomes grey.
Following the order on each square gives option A.

questions.question_text: Together the three squirrels Anni, Asia and Elli have 10 nuts. Each has a different number, at least 2. Anni has the least, Asia the most. How many nuts does Elli have?
questions.solution: Three different numbers, each at least 2, adding to 10 with Anni smallest and Asia largest: the only fit is 2, 3 and 5. So Anni = 2, Elli = 3, Asia = 5.
solution_steps: Need 3 different numbers, each ≥ 2, that add to 10.
The only such set is 2, 3, 5.
Anni (least) = 2, Asia (most) = 5, so Elli = 3.

questions.question_text: Each figure is made of 4 equally big cubes and coloured on the outside. Which figure needs the least amount of colour?
questions.solution: The more faces the cubes hide by touching each other, the less outside surface there is to colour. The compact 2-by-2 block (B) hides the most faces, so it needs the least colour.
solution_steps: Colour is needed only on the outside faces.
Cubes packed tightly together hide more faces.
The compact 2×2 block, option B, has the smallest outside surface.

questions.question_text: Four strips of paper are woven into a pattern. What do you see when you look at it from behind?
questions.solution: Looking from behind flips the pattern left-to-right (a mirror image). Mirroring the front weave gives option D.
solution_steps: Viewing from behind mirrors the picture left-to-right.
Flip the front weaving pattern horizontally.
The result is option D.
